info.text: In this demo I’ve imported the netnet standard library, created by Nick Briz both for the purposes of creating this website (netnet.studio) but also to help teach basic web programming concepts to creatives.
info.title: getting started
nn library
info.text: You'll see the nn library used across many of the lessons on this site, it provides methods to make it easier to work with the DOM (creating and modifying our HTML/CSS) as well as other helpers (loading/parsing data, color theory, music theory and other utilities like checking browser API support) meant to be used alongside other creative coding libraries.

info.title: mousemove
info.text: First we've created a function called drawGifs which we've attached to the window's mousemove event using the .on() method. This will call the function every time the mouse moves.

info.title: mouse down
info.text: Inside the function, the first line checks to see if the mouse is pressed down with nn.mouseDown. If the mouse is not currently pressed (the ! means not), we return which means we exit out of the function and do nothing. If it is pressed, then we continue.

info.title: create img
info.text: When the mouse is down, we create a new image element with nn.create('img') and then configure it through a chain of calls.

info.title: set src
info.text: The first chained method call is .set() which we can use to change any of the elements HTML attributes. Here we're setting its src attribute to the URL of the animated gif we want to create.

info.title: origin center
info.text: The next chained method call is .positionOrigin('center') which sets the transform origin so positions are calculated from the image center rather than its top-left corner (which is the default way CSS positions elements).

info.title: position
info.text: Next we call .position() passing nn.mouseX and nn.mouseY as it's position, which will place the image exactly under the cursor (relative to the center of the image).

info.title: no pointer
info.text: Then .css('pointer-events', 'none') ensures the new image won’t block future mouse events as you draw. Remember, we're creating new img elements here, so clicking over an existing one will behave as images do by default, they might get selected (highlited) and be draggable, this removes all that.

info.title: css method
info.text: We could modify any other CSS properties by either chaining more .css() calls or passing it an object with css properties and values.

info.title: addTo body
info.text: Lastly, we call .addTo('body') which inserts the element into the page.

info.title: recap
info.text: Together this acts like a simple GIF drawing tool, everytime the mouse moves we call our function, but only when the user is holding the mouse down do we create a new GIF and leave a trail.

info.title: lots more
info.text: There's loads more you can do with this library, you can find a list of all it's methods on the GitHub repo as well as plenty of other examples throughout this studio.
